body_txt: In some countries and time periods, women are expected to quit their jobs when they get married or at least when they have their first child. This can cause conflict if the woman loves her job and doesn't want to leave , but her husband and/or employer thinks she should. There are some male jobs that do not permit marriage, with the Latin-rite Catholic clergy being the best known example. note While priests in Orthodox Christianity (including the Eastern Catholic Churches) can remain married if they were already married before becoming a priest, any man who is already ordained as a priest cannot marry—even if widowed—and higher-ranking clergy cannot be married at all at any point after their elevation. However, this trope in media is normally Always Female , as men are expected to be the breadwinner of a couple . A more gender-balanced version is switching jobs because their current position is too hazardous or time-consuming. This is most common in Japanese works or Western works set before the 1970s. In modern-day Western works, a woman who quits to get married may be seen as a Gold Digger or Trophy Wife . The trope is moving toward Discredited Trope territory but hasn't quite gotten there yet. In older fiction, especially older SF , this is often an example of Values Dissonance . A woman who does this may have taken an MRS Degree in college ... or quit college so she could get married, which is another variant on this trope. This is a common outcome of the Family Versus Career or Career Versus Man choice.
